coredata.dc:description: The majority of research in the carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs) field has concentrated on the classification and synthesis of CNMs, with relatively few studies focusing on CNMs’ roles in photocatalytic hydrogen production. CNMs have proven their potential as an effective addition to the appeal of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution because of their superior chemical and physical properties. This paper explores the recent advancements in photocatalytic hydrogen (H2) production utilizing CNMs. As evidenced in the literature, carbon quantum dot (CQD)-sensitized titanium dioxide (TiO2) can demonstrate to have a photocatalytic hydrogen generation activity of 472 mol g−1 h−1 and 1458 mol g−1 h−1 without and with loading metal co-catalyst (Pt). The optimum catalyst, 0.4 CQD/CdS, contributes to the highest H2 production rate of 309 mmol g−1 h−1 (apparent quantum yield of 32.6%), which is 1.5 times greater than that of bare CdS. This would significantly accelerate the hydrogen production process. There are still challenges to reaching maximum photocatalytic hydrogen production, including low hydrogen storage. The overall price of hydrogen produced via photocatalysis is also higher because of the energy needed to store the hydrogen. Even though the problem is not directly related to the usage of CNMs, this restriction generates uncertainty and limits commercial investment. Given the rising demand for energy and the trend toward green power, it is recommended that extensive industrial uses of photocatalytic hydrogen produced by employing CNMs be investigated for better and more sustainable energy frameworks.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Zero-dimensional CNM (modified from Ref. [76]). Most zero-dimensional NMs are nanoparticles with a diameter of less than 100 nm that are quasi-spherical or spherical.

One-dimensional carbon nanotube (CNT) (modified from Ref. [76]). CNTs are unique “1D structures” that resemble rolled graphite single sheets (or more accurately graphene). This rolling can be performed at various curvatures and angles, producing nanotubes with distinct characteristics.

Two-dimensional CNM (graphene) (modified from Ref. [76]). These nanomaterials, in contrast to bulk materials, have a large number of atoms concentrated on their surface because of their higher aspect ratio (the ratio of their surface area to their volume). Since these atoms perform a distinct function from inside atoms, increasing the surface atoms causes a change in 2D nanomaterials' behaviour.
3D view of a CNM (diamond) (modified from Ref. [76]). Carbon-based nanomaterials with 3D structures and morphologies offer exceptional electrical, magnetic, and optical properties.

The roles of carbon materials in improving the photocatalytic activity for H2 production (modified from Ref. [70]). Carbon primarily contributes to the population increase of photo-carriers for photocatalytic reactions through its greater ability for exciton dissolution and improved electric conductivity for charge transport [105]. The charge carriers needed for H2 evolution activity are rarely generated by the increased light absorption that originates from carbon.
Illustration of the mechanism of H2 production via surface-functionalized CNTs, photocatalyst (CdS), and metal co-catalyst (Pt) (modified from Ref. [107]).
Bar chart illustrating the rate of evolution for ZnFe2O4 (ZFO) and ZnFe2O4/C60 photocatalyst [131].
The H2 production rate for WO3 (pure), 1% fullerene-WO3, 2% fullerene-WO3, 4% fullerene-WO3 and 7% fullerene-WO3 [112].
Potential use of CNMs in several photocatalytic H2 evolution pathways.
Photocatalytic hydrogen production rate using different carbon-based nanomaterials.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The majority of research in the carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs) field has concentrated on the classification and synthesis of CNMs, with relatively few studies focusing on CNMs’ roles in photocatalytic hydrogen production. CNMs have proven their potential as an effective addition to the appeal of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution because of their superior chemical and physical properties. This paper explores the recent advancements in photocatalytic hydrogen (H2) production utilizing CNMs. As evidenced in the literature, carbon quantum dot (CQD)-sensitized titanium dioxide (TiO2) can demonstrate to have a photocatalytic hydrogen generation activity of 472 mol g−1 h−1 and 1458 mol g−1 h−1 without and with loading metal co-catalyst (Pt). The optimum catalyst, 0.4 CQD/CdS, contributes to the highest H2 production rate of 309 mmol g−1 h−1 (apparent quantum yield of 32.6%), which is 1.5 times greater than that of bare CdS. This would significantly accelerate the hydrogen production process. There are still challenges to reaching maximum photocatalytic hydrogen production, including low hydrogen storage. The overall price of hydrogen produced via photocatalysis is also higher because of the energy needed to store the hydrogen. Even though the problem is not directly related to the usage of CNMs, this restriction generates uncertainty and limits commercial investment. Given the rising demand for energy and the trend toward green power, it is recommended that extensive industrial uses of photocatalytic hydrogen produced by employing CNMs be investigated for better and more sustainable energy frameworks.

ce:section.ce:para: The scientific field of nanotechnology is still emerging. Different categories of nanoparticles exist, including inorganic, organic, ceramic, and carbon-based nanoparticles [1]. Nanomaterials are also divided into different dimensions: zero, one, two and three dimensions [2]. The synthesis method and experimental conditions under which nanomaterials are utilized have a substantial impact on photocatalytic materials' performance [3]. The three fundamental chemical synthesis techniques are precipitation, photo-deposition, and chemical reduction [4]. In the same manner that carbon-based nanoparticles are subdivided into carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanofiber, graphene, carbon black, and fullerene, inorganic nanoparticles are subdivided into metal oxide nanomaterials and metal nanoparticles [5]. Nano-size photocatalysts have multifunctional features that have made it possible to use them effectively in applications for the energy, the environment, and healthcare sectors [4]. They are also well-known in the treatment of wastewater, especially for recovering heavy metals [6]. The process of enhancing photocatalytic H2 production has been investigated by several researchers. Yang et al. [7] synthesized ZnO-based flower structures using a one-step hydrothermal process. The activity of photocatalytic H2 generation was enhanced by the catalyst's large specific surface area and the charge transfer that occurred throughout the reaction. A significant hydrogen evolution rate was achieved by the TS-ZnO/carbon nanotubes (CNTs) photocatalyst when triethanolamine (C6H15NO3) was used as a sacrificial catalyst under illumination, which produced an impressive hydrogen evolution rate of 2683 mol g−1 h−1. Results from this research showed that the best sample had a yield of 729 mol g−1 h−1, which was around 17 times that of ZnS (2% ZnS/Cu3P). Under the simulation of solar light irradiation, the ideal ZnO@ZnS composite had high photocatalytic activity, producing 757.07 μmol g−1 of H2. This confirmed the initial hypothesis about enhanced charge transfer through the deposition of a suitable quantity of ZnS nanoparticles on ZnO nanorods.

In a similar study, Liang et al. [8] adopted the partial substitution technique, which is a straightforward and efficient way to reduce density and improve the thermally conductive qualities of polymer-based S–Al2O3 composites. The authors used three samples for analysis and the efficiency of filler-type thermal interface substances was enhanced by changing the filler's shape from platelet-like to stable spherical. The density of decreased globular-shaped flower-like graphene oxide (GFRGO)/S–Al2O3/PDMS composites with 1.0% graphene were approximately 2.49 g/cm3. GFRGO appeared to be a potential contender for improving the thermal conductivity of thermal interface materials that are lightweight and high-performing. However, contrary to this research, authors from other studies [9–11] presented contrasting opinions and showed that composites using graphene and boron nitride had attained greater values in heat conduction at high filler loading rates than flower-structured graphene oxides alone, opposing the claims of [8]. Liu et al. [12] argued that a dipolar energy breakdown in graphene oxide quantum dots/graphite quantum dots, by altering the ratio of oxygenated carbon to sp carbon, support earlier work conducted in the energy and material science fields, as opposed to the proposition made by Han et al. [13]. Epelle et al. [14] explored the role of nanomaterials by addressing the constraints posed by the synthesis and storage of hydrogen. The authors explained the latest developments in nanomaterial applications for storing hydrogen and presented a comparative analysis of the potential of several nanomaterials in storing hydrogen through the assessment of computational methodologies required for hydrogen retention. They also suggested outlining other substances such as complex clathrates and hydrides and strongly recommended utilizing nanomaterials for hydrogen generation and storage, which they believe paves way for future research directions [14]. The outstanding chemical and physical features of CNMs have shown them to be a promising addition to the appeal of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution.

CNMs have demonstrated a tremendous potential to improve the efficiency of semiconductor photocatalysts in past years because of their unique properties such as small size, higher adsorption, and active sites, band gap narrowing, co-catalysis, photosensitization, and photocatalysis. Recent relevant reviews on the roles of CNMs in improving photocatalytic hydrogen production were identified through a search. The search comprised the relevant keywords such as carbon nanomaterials, CNMs, photocatalytic, photocatalysis, hydrogen production, photocatalytic hydrogen production, and a combination of them. A good number of CNM-related reviews, including [15–21], were found during the search between 2019 and 2022. Even though all of these CNM studies contributed to noticeable advancements in the nanotechnology field, most of them have focused on the role of a particular CNM in improving photocatalytic hydrogen production. A relatively few studies reviewed all of the available CNMs in order to enhance photocatalytic hydrogen production. This paper places special emphasis on this research gap and sheds light on the potential of large-scale photolytic hydrogen production by appropriately utilizing different types and mechanisms of available CNMs. The present review will contribute to expanding the commercial applications of CNMs in the field of photocatalytic hydrogen production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics and functions of carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs)
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials offer exceptional electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties [22]. They have minimal toxicity, strong conductivity, excellent stability, and are environmentally friendly as they are constructed of pure carbon [23]. Carbon-based nanomaterials also have a high anisotropic heat conductivity [24]. This feature enables the use of CNMs in sophisticated computing electronics. CNMs are frequently used in biomedical fields like drug delivery, biosensors, imaging, cancer therapy, and tissue engineering [25]. However, their use continues to be affected by their toxicity to biological systems. The commonly used CNMs are discussed below.

Structurally, fullerenes can be defined as enclosed structures made up of a number of hexagon-shaped carbon structures with alternating single and double bonds and twelve pentagons with single bonds [16]. A new family of carbon-only compounds, fullerenes have a soccer-ball-shaped arrangement of 60 carbon atoms (C60) [26]. Carbon atoms form the building blocks of fullerene molecules, which take the form of a tube, hollow sphere, or ellipsoid. Although there are many other types of carbon, fullerenes have inevitably become the dominant zero-dimensional nanomaterial [27]. Fullerenes are stoichiometrically defined molecules with distinct electronic structures and properties in addition to unique molecular architectures [28]. However, these unique properties often make them fundamentally different from other CNMs, such as graphene nanosheets and carbon nanotubes, which are usually not considered molecules [28].

At room temperature, fullerenes have a face-cantered cubic structure with 111 and 200 crystal planes, and they are anisotropic [29]. With an increase in cage size, fullerenes become less reactive and soluble. They have desirable physical characteristics, including a 1.65 gcm−3 density, the ability to dissolve at 600 °C, and an inclination towards dissolving in organic solvents [30]. They also have high redox properties [31]. Their chemical properties include a high resonance stabilizing energy (810 kJmol-1), a CeC bond strength (444.4 kJmol-1), low toxicity, efficient absorption of organic contaminants, high electron transport, and strong antioxidant properties [32]. Pharmaceutical therapeutics, medicines, photovoltaics, and other biomedical applications elevate the activities of fullerene with a functionalized by-product [33].

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are carbon tubes with diameters on the nanometre scale [34]. Typical production processes for carbon nanotubes result in tubes that are significantly longer than they are wide [35]. This means that carbon nanotubes' length is often considered to be unlimited despite the fact that their end consequences are often disregarded. Because of their excellent adsorption capacity at ambient temperature, fine microspores, reduced mass density, stability of the chemical structure, and ease of synthesis from renewable feedstocks, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are considered as potential materials for H2 storage [36]. Single- and multi-walled CNT (SWCNT and MWCNT) are two different kinds of carbon nanotubes that are produced when a graphene sheet is rolled into a tube shape.

The exterior diameter ranges of the MWCNT and SWCNT are 0.4–2 nm and 2–100 nm, while their length is 0.2–5 μm [14]. Patel et al. [37] comprehensively investigated the SWCNT and MWCNT production processes' electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties. Due to their larger volume-to-surface ratio, MWCNTs can store more hydrogen than SWCNTs. Owing to their numerous layers, they are able to absorb hydrogen between different graphene sheets [38]. However, due to the enhanced purity's direct correlation with an increase in the number of hydrogen adsorbent surfaces accessible, the MWCNT's overall capacity to store hydrogen depends on the operational conditions and level of purity [14]. CNTs predominantly function as the composite sites for different MWCNTs because of their distinct structural, optical, and electronic characteristics and help to integrate with photoactive nanostructures to prevent the rearrangement of electron-hole pairs produced by light [39].

Graphene is a nanoscale allotrope of carbon that consists of a single layer of atoms organized in a hexagonal lattice [40]. In addition to being the strongest substance ever identified, graphene is also the thinnest at only one atom thick and extraordinarily strong, being roughly 200 times more durable than steel [41]. Graphene has remarkable light-absorbing properties and is an excellent thermal and electrical conductor [42]. The exceptional thermal, mechanical, optical, and electrical properties of graphene, a nanostructured two-dimensional (2D) macromolecular nanosheet of carbon atoms, has garnered considerable interest [43]. In particular, the electrical and optical properties prevent the rearrangement of light-generated electron-hole pairs, enhancing photocatalytic activity [39].

Graphene is distinct from other members of the carbon family due to its planar structure and hexagonal bonding patterns [44]. The surface area of graphene is typically condensed. The van der Waals force of attraction exists between graphite and graphene layers [16]. Strong interfacial interaction boosts the stiffness, elastic modulus, and mechanical strength of graphene nanoplatelets. Graphene can be mixed with many other substances (including metals and gases) to generate new compounds with unique and desirable characteristics [45]. When combined with glass fibers and polymers, graphene's conductivity increases [46]. Numerous innovative uses for graphene are on the horizon, including faster and more efficient electronics, accurate and efficient sensors, flexible displays, effective solar panels, quicker DNA sequencing, medication delivery, and many more [47]. 3D graphene's strong mechanical stability, electron conductivity, vast surface area, and quick electron transfer make it a good contender for photocatalytic H2 production [48].

The production of graphene oxide (GO) is the result of oxygen-containing groups being added to the graphite surface [49], which is accomplished by sonicating abrasion from stacked sheets of graphite oxide structure. GO is an insulating substance [50] because of the bonding of sp3 between the carbon atoms and the oxygen-containing groups. GO, like other 2D carbons, can be either single- or multilayer [51]. Although GO is a 2D carbon material, its characteristics are very different from those of graphene. It has a far lower electric conductivity than graphene and substantially more chemical activity, and it does not capture visible light [52]. The most current techniques for producing GO rely on the discernible intercalation capability of layered graphite.

GO can be made from graphite with an inter-planar distance of 0.80 nm using Hummer's method [53]. According to this method, graphite powder is changed into graphite oxide by adding NaNO3, H2SO4, H3PO4, and then KMnO4. Then, graphite oxide is changed into graphene oxide by sonication. rGO is produced when GO is reduced by a number of reducing agents, mainly hydrazine hydrate. As opposed to graphene, GO's valence and conduction bands are separated and its surface is hydrophilic [16]. Adding GO, which has a high thermal conductivity, to the latent functional thermal fluid makes the fluid much better at transmitting heat [54]. GO is often utilized to optimize oxide-containing catalysts' charge transfer processes by reducing the energy gap between the valence bond (VB) and conduction band (CB). Nitrogen-infused GO also helps to split photocatalytic water to produce hydrogen [55].

Nanodiamonds are truncated octahedral carbon NPs with a diameter of 2–8 nm [56]. Nanodiamonds, in contrast to graphites, have a layered, sphere-like structure. Three primary components of diamond nanoparticles' structure must be taken into account: overall shape, core, and surface [57]. The optical and magnetic properties of nanodiamonds make them special. Common applications for these materials include coating, abrasives, and semiconductors [58]. There is increasing demand for nanodiamonds owing to their unique thermal, mechanical and biological features, their vast surface area (more than 200 m2/g), and their hydrophilic surface due to the presence of numerous oxygenated functional groups [59]. An alternative emerging catalyst for activating persulfate is graphited nanodiamond (G-ND) [60]. These are inexpensive, simple to regenerate, and ecologically acceptable versions of nanodiamonds [61]. Explosive molecules, which serve as both a source of carbon and energy for conversion, can be used to create nanodiamonds [62].

A notable class of carbon compounds ranging in size from 1 to 10 nm is known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs). CQDs are a novel type of nano-carbons that have recently increased semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) popularity due to their many desirable optical properties, minimal toxicity, and low synthesis cost [63]. Clusters of carbon, along with other elements including nitrogen, sulphur, oxygen, and phosphorus, make up CQDs [64]. CQDs can be synthesized using organic molecules, naturally occurring products, and readily available bioresources; the processes are incredibly simple, cheap, and efficient [65]. They combine the distinctive electrical and optical characteristics of sp2 carbon materials. In order to manipulate high-performance photocatalysts, CQDs are a prime candidate since they can function both as a co-catalyst and a harvester of visible light [66]. The CQDs are good electron donors and acceptors and exhibit photo-harvester and conducting properties [67].

The exceptional ionic conductivity and nontoxicity of CQDs make them a popular choice for photocatalytic applications. A wide range of incident light is absorbed by CQDs when they are functionalized with other photocatalysts [68], which is crucial for photocatalysis. By separating the TiO2 electron-hole pairs, the CQDs act as a reservoir for electrons to increase efficiency [4]. By connecting with photocatalysts, CQDs can significantly increase photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency. Moreover, in water splitting reactions, CQDs function as a co-catalyst by trapping photocatalyst electrons and reducing H+ to H2. CQDs are promising materials in several applications including in the biomedical field due to their unique properties, such as fluorescence, biocompatibility, water solubility, small size, ease of alteration, low toxicity, reasonably priced scale-up production, and dynamic conjugation with several other nanoparticles [69]. Table 1 lists the potential application of CNMs in numerous processes for photocatalytic H2 evolution.

The study of zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials, which have many potential biosensing and biomedical applications, has exploded in popularity in recent years [72]. The materials that exist within the nanometre range in three-dimensional (3D) space are called zero-dimensional nanomaterials, e.g., fullerenes, atomic clusters, nanoparticles, and quantum dots. They are widely used in diagnosing diseases and detecting pathogens. Due to their smaller size and larger surface area to volume ratio, the effect of quantum confinement, decent biocompatibility, structural diversity, and suitable chemical and physical properties, their potential to be used in the therapeutics field and in energy conversion and production is regarded with great importance [73]. The two main categories of this class of 0D carbon-based nanomaterials are quantum dots with graphene (GQDs) and CQDs, both with sp2 hybridization [74]. Zhao & Xu [75] discussed the latest studies on co-catalysts for photocatalysis applications and highlighted their importance in amplifying the reaction efficiency using 0D carbon-based nanomaterials (CNM) as pollutant photo degradant. In Fig. 1 , the zero-dimensional (0D) view of a carbon allotrope called fullerene is presented [76].

Nanomaterials with one of their dimensions smaller than 100 nm are referred to as one-dimensional (1D) nanofillers. In contrast to its other two dimensions, one-dimensional nanomaterials (1D) have one dimension that is not at the nanoscale. 1D materials can be metallic, ceramic, or polymeric nanotube and nanorod filaments or fibre, nanofibres, and nanowires [77]. They often take the shape of sheets that range in thickness from a few nanometres to hundreds and with lengths of thousands of nanometres [78]. It is represented by the number 109 and results in the development of one-dimensional nanomaterials such as thin films. Such monolayers or thin films can be as small as 1 nm or as large as 100 nm. These nanomaterials are crucial for research and are utilized to create electronics, storage systems, and LEDs with nanoscale dimensions [1]. Due to their exceptional optical, thermal, magnetic, and electrical characteristics, they are extensively employed in microelectronics, biosensors, and sensors [78]. One-dimensional carbon nanotubes have been reportedly found to have better dispersibility as well [79]. Fig. 2 illustrates the one-dimension (1D) version of a CNM [76].

Two-dimensional (2D) materials show promise as alternative photocatalysts because of their sheet-like structures, mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties, as well as the operational benefits of being easy to handle. They also have higher melting points than other nanomaterials and can be included in composites with highly photoactive co-catalysts [80]. Even though all 2D nanomaterials have distinctly diverse compositions and crystal structures, they may all be classified as both stratified and unlayered two-dimensional nanomaterials. In layered nanomaterials, atoms in the plane are bound together by strong bonds. Each layer's chemical linkages are built up to bulk structures through van der Waals's force of contact [81].

To date, a number of 2D materials, including graphitic carbon nitride, graphene, hexagonal boron nitride, and black phosphorene/phosphorus have been identified. All of these compounds have demonstrated photocatalytic water splitting activity potential. Unusual structural, electrical, and optical characteristics can be found in 2D materials. However, some reported constraints on reaction completion have arisen as a result of the recombination rate of the electrons and holes that carry out reactions. As an alternative to traditional metal-based systems, compound photocatalysts and metal-free components have recently been developed. Examples that stand out in the literature are red-P, boron, and alpha-sulphur. These fundamental photocatalysts, however, have little photocatalytic activity. Researchers use a variety of methods to significantly increase activity, including doping, sensitization, tweaking morphology, and creating combined structures [80]. A 2D perspective of a well-known carbon allotrope called ‘graphene’ is shown in Fig. 3 .

The outstanding optical and electrical features of 2D materials demonstrate their ability to confine electrons in their ultrathin layer. As a result of electron confinement, the optical and electrical properties of 2D materials are distinct from those of conventional materials [82]. Numerous heterojunctions and heterostructures can develop on the strong in-plane covalent bonding sites. A high specific surface area of the 2D architecture also facilitates surface reactions like water splitting [83]. A viable approach to creating hydrogen utilizing a quick and efficient process is the photocatalytic water splitting method. Recent developments in 2D nanomaterials (NMs) and composite heterostructures-based photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) were reported by Ganguly et al. [83]. These materials were found to be advantageous for surface-related applications due to their potent in-plane chemical bonding and mild van der Waals interaction. The authors explored the modern protocols created for the synthesis of 2D NMs and highlighted the significance of doping and structural flaws in the electronic structure.

Three-dimensional (3D) is used to describe materials that can exist at any size on the nanometre scale. Hybrid 3D-porous carbon-based nanomaterials are excellent for biological research, wearable sensors, and energy storage and conversion [84]. In many applications, H2 is regarded as an alternative source of energy for sustainable energy systems. Due to its excellent electron conductivity, quick electron transfer, mechanical stability, and huge surface area, 3D graphene, one of carbon's allotropes, is one of the most viable choices for a variety of applications, particularly in photocatalytic H2 production. Since a three-dimensional structure may improve photocatalytic performance by increasing the accessibility of the active site, enhancing light absorption, and improving charge transfer, it is important to examine the differences in physical properties between dimensions [48].

In the case of carbon nitride, the 3D structure gives it more catalytic active sites and a greater specific surface area, which is good for transferring charge and mass to trigger photocatalytic hydrogen generation events [85]. Lee et al. [71] proposed monolithic 3D nanostructures which offer a maximum surface area to volume ratio conducting energy photolysis with utmost efficiency. The authors developed advanced photocatalyst models to provide highly effective TiO2 photocatalysts. Utilizing heterostructures made of carbon-based nanomaterials allows for efficient TiO2 photocatalysts while maximizing the use of the solar spectrum and charge separation. Fig. 4 depicts the 3D representation of carbon in its allotropic form (diamond).

Hydrogen is one of the most ideal alternatives to non-renewable energy. Despite the high energy density and abundance of hydrogen as a resource, the thermal processes used for H2 generation outweigh the advantages [86]. Hydrogen can also be generated using thermochemical processes, direct solar water splitting processes, electrolytic processes, and biological processes. Hydrogen production using alternative routes has been developed over the past years. Hydrogen production via photocatalysis of water has been studied widely and is receiving attention due to its eco-friendly nature. Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution consists of a multi-layered chemical and physical process. The process for photocatalytic H2 production is divided into: (i) light absorption; (ii) excitation and transmission of electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB); (iii) separation and transfer of the electron-hole pairs to the surface; and (iv) the absorbed H+ is reduced by the electrons in a conduction band to form H2 [87]. The oxidation of the water into oxygen is carried out by the holes in the VB.

Certain limitations hamper photocatalytic efficiency. The narrow light absorption range due to the large band gap of the existing photocatalysts has been identified as one of the biggest challenges in improving photocatalytic performance. Additionally, the recombination of electron holes acts as an obstacle because the recombination of photo carriers on the photocatalyst's surface leads to reduced H+ [88]. Through decades of research, only a handful of stable photocatalysts have been established which are commonly used for hydrogen evolution. Photocatalysts such as TiO2, WO3, MoS2, CdS, g-C3N4, Cu2O, ZnIn2S4, and CdIn2S4 are some of the widely used catalysts. Semiconductors with wide band gaps display lower solar to hydrogen conversion rates due to their inability to efficiently utilize visible light. In contrast, semiconductors with smaller band gaps efficiently absorb visible light but exhibit poor oxidation capacity. Efficient proton reduction sites which achieve higher H2 production are possible by increasing the absorption capacity of the semiconductors [89].

New research on improving the photocatalytic efficiency for hydrogen production introduces alternate strategies. Strategies such as localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), doping (element) co-catalyst utilization, and fabrication of the heterojunction have been studied [88]. Due to the LSPR property modified semiconductors display high efficiency in photocatalysis [90]. With a higher light trapping capacity, plasmonic photocatalysis exhibits a potential for water splitting making it an effective alternative. Similarly, Wang et al. [91] utilized platinum oxide (PtO) as a co-catalyst for the production of hydrogen. The PtO was coupled with g-C3N4 to study hydrogen production capacity. The study demonstrated that the coupling of the semiconductor with the co-catalyst platinum oxide displays higher H2 production activity alongside higher photostability. New research also takes resource availability, cost, and efficiency into consideration. There has been a switch from precious metal to transition metal phosphides because of their high availability and low cost. TMPs serve as both co-catalyst and host photocatalysts [92].

Rapid industrialization alongside the depletion of non-renewable resources threatens the world with environmental pollution and oncoming looming energy crisis. Finding environmentally friendly and effective remedies to these persistent problems is of the utmost importance. Photocatalysis is a chemical transformation driven by the process of natural photosynthesis [93]. To simplify, photocatalysis can be separated into two terms, ‘photo’ and ‘catalyst’. The term ‘photo’ is associated with photon light while ‘catalyst’ refers to the component utilized to alter the reaction process. Photocatalysis is one type of chemical reaction that occurs when light energy and catalysts are present. According to Ameta et al. [94], the photocatalysts used in photocatalysis are semiconductors. The classification of semiconductors is carried out by evaluating the band gap, which is the difference in energy between the conduction band (CB) and the valence band (VB). Semiconductors conduct electricity and when sufficient energy (light of the required wavelength) is available, the electron (e−) in the valence shell absorbs it and the electron jumps to the conducting band. The electron jump creates a hole (h+) in the valence shells resulting in a hole-electron pair formation which then enables a potential redox reaction. When these semiconductors come in contact with light an electron pair is generated.

While the photocatalysis reaction with conventional conductors is well-known, there is a surge in the invention of new conductors. Alternatives such as plasmonic metal, 2D materials, and quantum dots have been utilized for effective photocatalysis [93]. Photocatalysis uses metal oxides, nitrides, and sulfide-based photocatalysts. Photocatalysts like zinc oxide (ZnO), titanium dioxide (TiO2), cerium (IV) oxide (CeO2), zirconium dioxide (ZrO2), tin (IV) oxide (SnO2), antimony tetroxide (Sb2O4), zinc sulfide (ZnS), and cadmium sulfide (CdS) have been utilized. The performance of these metal-based semiconductors can be improved through modification [95]. The main processes of improving the performance of the semiconductors are: (i) forming the localized state above the VB or forming the localized state below the CB, (ii) using a conductor with a lesser band gap, (iii) forming colour centre, and (iv) surface modification. Techniques such as doping, co-doping, coupling, sensitization and substitution can be implemented for performance enhancement [94]. However, despite the progress in the synthesis of visible light-active materials, certain limitations hamper performance. An alternative polymerics carbon nitrate (PCN) for photocatalysis has been studied for its potential application.

HER is a chemical process that produces hydrogen. It followed a series of steps performed on an electrode's surface [96]. The activity of HER is governed by three significant parameters: (i) active sites' availability, (ii) activated complex energetics resulting in H2 generation, and (iii) proton donor's nature, which can be either hydronium or water [97]. The production of hydrogen from protons often necessitates the use of reducing equivalents and a catalyst [98]. Hydrogen is absorbed in the catalyst surface during the electrocatalytic process known as the hydrogen evolution reaction. The energy of adsorption differs depending on the electrode material and the kinetics of the hydrogen evolution reaction [99]. The electrochemical HER can occur in both alkaline and acidic solutions [100]. Redox reaction occurs at the electrodes/electrolyte interface. The electrolyte's pH value in the solution influences H2 generation [101]. However, HER can be an undesirable side reaction that hinders other reductions like carbon dioxide's electrochemical reduction and nitrogen fixation [102].

In acidic media, a hydrogen evolution reaction is carried out in two successive steps on the catalyst's surface. The steps involved are electrochemical hydrogen absorption and electrochemical/chemical hydrogen desorption reaction. Initially, a Volmer reaction occurs where the H+ is absorbed on the catalyst's surface to form a hydrogen atom (H∗) by (Eq. (1)). Here H∗ denotes the active site on the catalyst's surface. In the next step, H∗, hydrogen +, and the electron combine leading to the H2 formation molecule, a step known as the Heyrovsky step (Eq. (2)). Alternatively, instead of the Heyrovsky step, the Tafel step occurs for the formation of H2. Unlike the Heyrovsky step, instead of combining with H+ and the electron, the H∗ combines with another H∗ on the surface of the catalyst. The combination of two H∗ forms the H2 molecule (Eq. (3)) [101].

Equations:
(1) H+ + e− → H∗
(2) H∗ + H+ + e− → H2
(3) 2H∗ → H2
The overall equation for the acidic media is 2H+ + 2e- → H2

In an alkaline medium, the hydrogen evolution reaction rate is slower than in an acidic one. The hydrogen evolution reaction in an alkaline medium follows a different pathway to H2 formation. The lack of protons in the alkaline medium leads to the dissociation of H2O molecules in Volmer (Eq. (4)) and Heyrovsky (Eq. (5)) steps to provide protons [103]. According to Zhao et al. [101], the balance between the hydrogen adsorption energy and the energy needed for dissociation influences the hydrogen evolution reaction activity.

Equations:
(4) H2O + e + ∗ = H∗+OH-
(5) H2O + e + H∗ = H2+OH-
The overall equation for hydrogen evolution reaction in alkaline media is defined as 2H2O + 2e- = 2H2 + 2OH-

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recent advancements in photocatalytic hydrogen production using various CNMs
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic solar water splitting splits the water molecules into O2 and H2 via a redox reaction. Photocatalysis starts with photo adsorption; semiconductors must have a befitting band gap to harvest solar energy [70]. According to the Nernst equation, it was determined that the minimum energy required for water splitting is 1.23 eV. Suitable semiconductors for photocatalysis reactions must have a band gap of approximately 2eV [104]. While numerous semiconductors have been established for photocatalytic hydrogen production, very few of the semiconductors are suitable for practical applications [70]. The limitations of existing semiconductors, such as poor absorbed light and high recombination, must be overcome to achieve an efficient outcome. Recent studies emphasize the need to find a cost-effective alternative that improves the light absorption range and redox potential for the photocatalytic reaction.

Carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs) have emerged as efficient catalysts due to their unique properties. Large surface area, suitable morphology with chemical stability, and thermal stability make CNMs suitable for photocatalysis [16]. CNMs can be utilized for multiple applications. According to Cao and Yu [70], carbon materials can be used in numerous ways (Fig. 5 ), such as supporting material, photocatalyst, or co-catalyst. They can also enhance the absorption capacity, increase the number of active sites, electron acceptors, and transporter channels, promote photosensitization, and ensure the effect of band gap narrowing. These multiple benefits of carbon-based semiconductors enhance photocatalysis performance. Additionally, carbon-based materials can be modified to obtain higher efficiency. Modification methods such as doping, surface functionalization, and interface engineering have all been successfully implemented.

CNMs, including carbon quantum dots, fullerenes, graphene, graphene oxide, and carbon nanotubes have been successfully utilized to enhance hydrogen production [106]. The current objective is to ensure an overall cleaner hydrogen production utilizing CNMs. The use of carbon-based materials for photocatalytic activity has been actively studied to make photocatalytic hydrogen production feasible at a larger scale. Cost efficiency, desirable properties, and potential minimization of environmental burden have led to numerous studies on H2 production using CNMs.

The utilization of CNMs in improving photocatalytic H2 generation is considered a significant breakthrough. According to Singla et al. [16], photocatalytic water splitting has been demonstrated to be feasible with carbon-based nanomaterials due to their pore volume, large surface area, thermal and chemical durability, and attractive shape. After reviewing 180 articles and using machine learning techniques to examine a dataset with 10,560 data points collected from 584 experiments on n-type semiconductors in photoelectrochemical water splitting, the authors deduced that 0.07 mol Dy3+: ZTO was the most effective nano-photocatalyst for hydrogen synthesis. Under ideal loading conditions, the Ni-salen molecules’ maximum H2 evolution rate reached 23.3 mmol g−1 h−1. However, they did not consider the issues linked to the limited applications of hydrogen evolution produced by metal-free catalysts through the splitting of water. Carbon-based photocatalysts have also piqued the curiosity of many researchers for visible-light-driven hydrogen evolution processes.

Recently developed systems focus on high efficiency and cost-effectiveness. Carbon nanotubes have numerous characteristics that exhibit huge potential to enhance photocatalytic efficiency. The hydrophobicity and modification potential via surface functionalization makes them suitable for a wide range of applications. CNTs modified by acid functionalization can improve dispersion capability and enhance photocatalytic efficiency via surface active site improvement [107]. The structuring of semiconductors with carbon nanotubes increases its potential for practical applications [108]. Alongside photocatalytic efficiency and a wider range of applications, developing systems that produce hydrogen from water at a low cost is important for large-scale implementation.

Engineering carbon-based nanomaterials have the potential to become a sustainable alternative for hydrogen production [22]. According to Gopannagari et al. [107], the multi-walled CNTs on the CdS hybrid display an increase in photocatalytic efficiency. Additionally, this study also investigated the influence of secondary surface functionalized CNTs on the CdS for the hydrogen evolution reaction. The highest hydrogen production rate was achieved by ascorbic acid functionalized CNTs with integrated PT and a CdS nanohybrid (Pt-Af CNT/CdS). Fig. 6 illustrates the surface functionalized CNTs with the semiconductor photocatalyst (CdS) and metal co-catalyst (Pt). This modification of the functional group on the CNT depicted additional advantages such as improved photoactivity and photostability of the semiconductor (CdS), which proved to be an advantage for H2 reproduction.

Recent developments have produced systems that are both highly efficient and cost-effective. Liu et al. [109] developed bamboo-structured nitrogen to carbon nanotube encapsulated with metal nanoparticles (cobalt). This was then coupled with the photocatalyst graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) to form a photocatalyst/co-catalyst system. The g-C3N4/CNCNT improves the photocatalytic activity with an increased H2 evolution rate of 1208 μmol g−1 h−1 in the presence of triethanolamine. Similarly, the integration of titanium oxide (TiO2) inside the carbon nanotubes was also studied to investigate its influence on hydrogen production [108]. This combination was developed for higher efficiency in UV and visible light absorption, minimizing the band gap, and reducing the photo-induced charge combination. At the mark of 50 h of solo irradiation, a 38.66 mmol g−1 h−1 H2 production rate was observed. The increase in H2 production rate was around 1.5-fold higher compared to the solely TiO2-based photocatalyst.

Feng et al. [110] utilized CNTs in the preparation of a g-C3N4/CNTs/CdZnS Z-scheme photocatalyst, and they evaluated its performance in photocatalytic hydrogen generation. g-C3N4/CNTs/CdZnS was found to have very good stability as a photocatalyst for hydrogen generation. In terms of photocatalytic hydrogen production, the produced g-C3N4/CNTs/CdZnS demonstrated substantial improvement over CdZnS, single-phase g-C3N4, as well as biphasic g-C3N4/CdZnS photocatalysts. Since CNTs act as an electron mediator, they speed up the recombination of photogenerated holes in the valence band of g-C3N4 and photogenerated electrons in the conduction band of CdZnS. This enables the g-C3N4/CNTs/CdZnS Z-scheme photocatalyst to more easily escape the photogenerated electrons, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production performance. This study [110] theoretically justifies the use of CNTs in Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts as intermediate electron mediators. As a result of the development of the Z-scheme heterojunction system, the lifespan of photogenerated carriers and separation efficiency are greatly enhanced, allowing g-C3N4/CNTs/CdZnS to maintain its stable and efficient hydrogen production.

Graphene has been studied as a substitute for noble metal co-catalysts. Graphene, a 2D material composed of carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice and held together by special chemical bonds called SP2, has been shown to be an effective semiconductor. Graphene displays high electron mobility, conductivity, and a large specific surface area which serves as an advantage over the existing noble metal co-catalysts [111]. Tungsten trioxide (WO3) synthesized with graphene via a hydrothermal process was studied by Ref. [112] for its effectiveness in hydrogen evolution. The properties of graphene facilitate electron transfer which makes it favourable for photocatalysis. The WO3/graphene system was investigated for photocatalytic production of H2 with a varying percentage of graphene content in WO3 [112]. The WO3/graphene system with 7% graphene content exhibited a very high rate of H2 evolution of around 288 μmol h−1 g−1. High efficiency and low cost make graphene a suitable alternative catalyst in hydrogen production.

Three-dimensional graphene has also been studied to assess its role in improving photocatalytic activity. Wang et al. [113] constructed a three-dimensional CdS nanosphere/graphene in order to enhance photocatalytic activity. The hydrothermal self-assembly approach was used to construct the 3D networks. The accumulation and stacking of the graphene nanosheets are common in 2D graphene nanosheets which leads to a decline in active sites, conductivity, and diffusion. The 3D networks effectively tackle these limitations and improve the conductivity while maintaining electrical properties. Because of their linked nature, graphene networks facilitate an electron channel that aids in the segregation of photogenerated electrons and holes. One major barrier is the loss of photocatalytic activity due to the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The facilitation of photoexcited electron transport to co-catalyst (Pt) placed on the graphene network. With 2 wt% of graphene networks, a hydrogen production rate of 2310 μmol g−1 h−1 was achieved.

The existing limitations of graphene are overcome by further modification. TiO2 and effective semiconductors are often coupled semiconductors with a lower band gap to improve photocatalytic performance [114]. TiO2-graphene composite has excellent properties which exhibit immense potential for photocatalysis. The existing limitation of TiO2/graphene composite's poor electron storage capacity can hamper photocatalytic performance. Khan et al. [115] doped TiO2 with europium (Eu) to improve the adsorption capacity. The Eu-TiO2/graphene composite was investigated for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The photocatalytic performance for TiO2-graphene, Eu- TiO2 and Eu- TiO2/graphene was enhanced compared to the pure TiO2. The highest H2 evolution rate of 100 mmol g−1 h−1 was observed for Eu- TiO2/graphene doped with 2 at%. Sahani et al. [116] discussed the potential of graphene derivatives and carbon dots as solid electron mediators and noble metal-free co-catalysts in the hydrogen evolution reaction. They explained the basics, developments, and importance of layered double hydroxides in various photocatalytic processes. The increased photoactivity was linked to the creation of a Z-scheme from LaCoO3/PCN supported by silver nanoparticles (Ag-NPs). The optical bandgap was found to slightly increase as a result of the integration of GNP on the TNT arrays.

The presence of graphene improves light absorption capacity and ensures effective charge separation. Chang et al. [117] proposed a mechanism of water splitting and glycerol reformation reaction by photocatalysis. Zinc oxide (ZnO) and zinc sulfide (ZnS) photocatalysts utilize sacrificial reagents to enhance H2 production. The synthesized ZnO–ZnS/graphene composite with sacrificial reagent glycerol solution was investigated for the impact of the composite on photocatalytic activity and inhibition of carrier charge recombination. It was determined that ZnO–ZnS/graphene composite exhibits an improved hydrogen production rate owing to the graphene. With an optimum glycerol concentration of 40 vol% the hydrogen generation rate was observed up to 1070 μmol g−1 h−1. The introduction of graphene can also be effective in utilizing by-products like glycerol leading to the minimization of waste.

Similar to other nanomaterials, graphene oxide (GO) exhibit a wide range of properties such as high optical transmittance, large surface area and convenient career mobility [118]. Graphene oxide's adaptability and ease of modification have made it a popular choice as a semiconductor support material. The structure of graphene oxide has the unique properties to produce hetero-composite materials. The 2D hexagonal structure sheet has oxygen functional groups dispersed in the nanosheets. Additionally, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) has also been studied to assess its conductivity and potential to incorporate with semiconductors. Unlike regular GO, rGO has the oxygen functional groups removed. Both GO and rGO have been effectively utilized to enhance photocatalytic applications.

Semiconductors have been modified with GO to improve photocatalytic activity and improve the hydrogen production rate. Quiroz-Cardoso et al. [119] studied CdS semiconductors modified with GO and nickel (Ni) nanomaterials to assess the potential increase in photoactivity. Pure CdS semiconductors have poor efficiency because of the fast recombination of electron-hole pairs, modification with graphene is likely to minimize the recombination. The presence of an oxygen functional group on the surface of graphene oxide nanosheets allows further modification through the integration of additional semiconductors. In sacrificial reagent ethanol solution, the composite Ni/GO-CdS exhibited high photocatalytic activity increasing H2 production by up to 8866 μmol g−1 h−1 which was 6.3 times that of bare CdS.

rGO, produced by a reduction of GO, has a higher number of defects due to the presence of oxygen-containing functionalities. The oxygen content of reduced graphene oxide heavily influences its electrical properties [120]. Numerous combinations of various semiconductors/rGO composites have been studied to determine the highest efficiency in photocatalytic hydrogen production. Using the facile microwave ignition method, nickel titanate (NiTiO3) and reduced graphene oxide were synthesized to form a composite [121]. It was observed that increasing graphene content to the optimum level (5% graphene) exhibited a significant increase in performance by obtaining a hydrogen generation rate of 8383 μmol g−1 h−1. Increasing the graphene content to more than the optimum level leads to a decrease in photocatalytic performance. NiTiO3 and graphene display synergistic effects that efficiently utilized the solar energy by extending the absorption capacity of visible light.

Since photocatalysis relies on solar light and an appropriate semiconductor, it can play a significant role among the several alternatives to produce hydrogen. In the study conducted by Rivero et al. [122], the efficiency of binary and ternary photocatalysts (TiO2/Pt, TiO2/rGO, and TiO2/rGO/Pt) produced for H2 generation was evaluated based on the charge-carrying capacity of rGO and the efficiency of platinum NPs as traps for photogenerated electrons. When rGO is combined with TiO2, the H2 production rate increases by nearly two times (1.95) as much as when using TiO2 alone. The hydrogen production rate was also increased by a factor of 15.26 using a binary TiO2/Pt photocatalyst as opposed to pure TiO2. However, the ternary catalyst underperformed the binary TiO2/Pt catalyst, perhaps because of inefficient co-catalyst ratios. The trade-off between cost and catalyst performance is interesting to investigate because it is reduced by the inclusion of rGO.

The rGO has also been used as a support material for ternary hybrid semiconductors. Hafeez et al. [123] synthesized g–C3N4– dTiO2/rGO nanocomposite to assess the production rate of hydrogen in sacrificial agent glycerol. The study concluded that the mass ratio of 70:30 of g-C3N4 and TiO2 in nanocomposite leads to high H2 production activity (23,143 mmol g−1 h−1). Similarly, metallic sulfide, Cd0.5Zn0.5S, with a g-C3N4 nanomaterial in the presence of rGO was also studied [124]. The rGO served as a key component in the ternary nanocomposite system as it bridges the two semiconductors. Hydrogen generation of 39.24 mmol g−1 h−1 in the absence of metal co-catalyst was obtained by the ternary system.

The potential synthesis of CQDs via the green and low-cost route makes it suitable for large scale implementation. Sargin et al. [125] synthesized CQDs from Agaricus bisporus, an edible species of mushroom. The luminescent properties of CQDs enable them to serve the role of photosensitisers for the semiconductor TiO2. The research looked into CQD-sensitized TiO2's photocatalytic hydrogen production capability with and without a metal-based co-catalyst. The CQD-sensitized TiO2 exhibited high photocatalytic activity of 472 μmol g−1 h−1. However, while using the metal co-catalyst (Pt), the photocatalytic hydrogen production was even higher, reaching up to 1458 μmol g−1 h−1. Similarly, Gogoi et al. [126] synthesized carbon quantum dots from orange peel and integrated them into the surface of a CdS nanowire via the chemical adsorption method. In presence of the sacrificial reagents Na2S/Na2SO3, an H2 production rate of 309 mmol g−1 h−1 was achieved. A combination of sulphur vacancies and cadmium oxide formed as a result of CQDs interacting with CdS, resulting in significant photocatalytic activity.

The simultaneous facilitation of electron transport and suppression of carrier charge recombination is vital for obtaining efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. Wang et al. [127] utilized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with g-C3N4 to improve electron transfer. Implantation of tubular CQDs on g-C3N4 results in the formation of micro-regional heterostructures and morphology structure change. An evident increase in photocatalytic activity was observed under visible light irradiation, and a 3538.3 mmol g−1 h−1 H2 production rate was recorded. Additionally, the performance of CQD/semiconductor composites can be further improved by the addition of metal atoms such as zinc (Zn), bismuth (Bi), cobalt (Co), cadmium (Cd), and titanium (Ti) [128]. The doped CQD/CdS nanocomposites exhibit better performance in comparison to the undoped composite. Among the metal atoms, doped composite Bi-CQD/CdS promote the highest electron transfer efficiency with an H2 production rate of 1.77 mmol g−1 h−1. Han et al. [13] discovered that carbon dots (CDs) with a narrow bandwidth emission result from homogeneous size distribution. Based on this innovative SnO2 electron transport layer, manufactured planar perovskite solar cells show a remarkable increase in efficiency. These studies on the structure design and comprehension of the photophysical characteristics of CDs present new directions for the creation of high-performance afterglow materials.

The need for more stable and effective hydrogen-evolving photocatalysts is crucial. The strategy proposed by Meng et al. [129] can be regarded as an innovative approach to producing uniform photocatalytic cocatalysts. For photocatalytic H2 production, CdS nanorods (NRs) were loaded with Co@NC (Co NPs surrounded by N2-doped carbon layers) generated from a combination of CQDs and Co2+ as cocatalyst [129]. These experiments revealed that CdS with a Co@NC loading amount of 3 wt% (CdS-3) has a hydrogen evolution activity that is 21.8 mmol g−1h−1 and is an increase of 29.8 times compared to CdS alone. At 420 nm, an apparent quantum efficiency of 41.8% was achieved, which was superior to that of CdS-3. Electrochemical measurements and photoluminescence spectra demonstrated that efficient electron transfer between the CdS NRs and Co@NC is responsible for the hybrid nanostructure's improved photocatalytic performance.

With its narrow band gap and 3D structure, fullerene (C60) serves as an outstanding electron acceptor and facilitates electron transportation. Modification using fullerene and its derivatives has exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance [130]. Several studies have been carried out to investigate the influence of fullerene modification on semiconductors. Behera and Parida [131] synthesized ZnFe2O4 (ZFO) and ZnFe2O4/C60 nanocomposites to assess photocatalytic performance enhancement. Both nanocomposites were synthesized via the hydrothermal method. X-ray diffraction (XRD) and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) analyses were used to observe their crystalline formation, recombination, and separation characteristics. Nanocomposites modified with C60 displayed higher crystallinity and low recombination due to the presence of better separation properties. There was an improvement in photocatalytic activity. Fig. 7 depicts the difference in the H2 evolution rate for both nanocomposites. The H2 evolution rate for ZnFe2O4/C60 was 450.75 μmol g−1 h−1, which was substantially greater than that of the pure ZnFe2O4 nanocomposite.

CNMs are largely employed to construct heterostructures due to their excellent properties. Ding et al. [132] modified the SnO2 semiconductor with C60 to assess the influence of C atoms in the interfacial interaction. Three different modifications of the fullerene heterostructures using varying carbon atoms were investigated for photocatalytic activity. The increasing number of C atoms in heterostructures C20/SnO2, C26/SnO2 and C60/SnO2 led to decreasing band gap. Enhancing the light absorption capacity improves photocatalytic activity. Bilal et al. [112] synthesized fullerene-WO3 to investigate the rate of H2 evolution after modification with C60. The 4% fullerene-WO3 composite exhibits low PL intensity which suggests effective charge separation, achieving the highest H2 production rate (>150 mmol g−1 h−1). Fig. 8 illustrates the rate of H2 production of prepared photocatalyst composites with all the fullerene modified WO3 composites depicting a higher rate of H2 evolution compared to bare WO3.

There can be further improvement in the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution of semiconductor/C60 nanocomposites through additional chemical compounds. Song and Zhang [133] synthesized g-C3N4/C60 which lead to a remarkable increase in photocatalytic performance. The sample with 15 mg/L C60 aqueous solution GC15 obtained the highest H2 evolution rate of 2268.6 μmol/g after the 10-h mark (227 μmol g−1 h−1). For further enhancement, K2HPO4 (0.2 mol/L) was added to the system and the synergetic reaction between proton reduction and triethanolamine (TEOA) photooxidation doubled the H2 evolution amount to 4161 μmol/g. Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution using CNM-enhanced nanocomposites can be improved by further research. Rates of photocatalytic hydrogen generation from various carbon-based nanomaterials are summarized in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges and benefits of photocatalytic hydrogen production using carbon-based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: CNMs are widely employed for photocatalytic reactions in the sectors of environmental protection and energy conversion due to their favourable catalytic performance and low cost [136]. Hybrid semiconductor photocatalysts based on carbon are superior to pure semiconductor photocatalysts in their ability to generate hydrogen [137]. Despite the emergence of new technologies and methods, there are still challenges in achieving maximum photocatalytic hydrogen production. Several factors, including charge-carrier recombination, interfacial charge transfer inhibition, degradation efficiency, and charge separation, reduce the effectiveness of photocatalysis process when exposed to the visible spectrum [138]. One of the prominent challenges emphasized is low hydrogen storage [83]. Low volumetric hydrogen must be compressed to form liquid or gas to be stored. The energy required to store hydrogen adds to the overall cost of photocatalytic H2 production [139]. While the challenge does not directly arise from the usage of CNMs, this limitation creates uncertainty and constraints on a commercial investment. Even with multi-functionality, there are certain limitations of each carbon material that hinder its wide-scale application [70].

While CNMs are cheap, the process of production may not be economically viable. The high cost and energy demand associated with the preparation and application of CNMs limits their commercialization and implementation at a larger scale. The synthesis of CNMs remains a challenge that can be only resolved through further research. There is a need to determine the most feasible and effective method for bulk production. Since this is an emerging technology, there is uncertainty and a lack of clarity about particular ideas. The existing information on morphology, composition, and interfacial modification may not be adequate, but as our knowledge is constantly evolving, a better understanding of CNMs can be developed.

Due to their exceptional physical and chemical properties, CNMs have secured their potential as an effective addition to the enchantment of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. Recent advancements in heterostructures and hybrid applications contribute to a significant increase in photocatalytic H2 production efficiency. Nanomaterial composites and their different modifications effectively overcome the existing limitations that negatively impact photocatalytic performance. Interfacial changes and fabrication of nanomaterial successfully impede recombination and improve carrier transport contributing to the enhanced rate of H2 evolution. Nanomaterials can easily be integrated with semiconductors due to their cost-effectiveness and excellent properties. Additionally, a new synthesis method of nanomaterial preparation can further improve visible-light absorption and lead to higher photostability [83]. Facile synthesis and cost-effectiveness make CNMs easy to commercialize. Ongoing research highlights the need for sustainable and feasible methods that ensure the convenient production of CNMs. Additionally, photocatalytic hydrogen production can use alternative raw materials such as bio-waste, liquid waste and glycerol instead of water [140]. Table 3 summarizes the benefits and challenges associated with the use of nanomaterials in photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Recent advances in the use of hybrid nanostructures have significantly increased the efficiency of photocatalytic H2 generation. Interfacial modifications and nanomaterial production successfully inhibit recombination and improve carrier transport, contributing to an accelerated rate of H2 evolution. This paper reviewed the progress of CNMs in enhancing hydrogen generation through photocatalysis. The highest H2 production rate was calculated for the optimum catalyst, 0.4 CQD/CdS, which produced H2 at a rate of 309 mmol g−1 h−1 (apparent quantum produce of 32.6%). This production rate was 1.5 times more than that of bare CdS. The thermal conductivity of reduced globular-shaped graphene oxide composites with a density of approximately 2.49 g/cm3 and 1.0% graphene can be enhanced by 48% which is approximately 2.1 times higher than that of other composites.

Even though carbon materials are multifunctional, they have several restrictions that prevent their widespread application. One of the most prominent issues presented is the scarcity of hydrogen storage. In order to store hydrogen, it must be compressed into a liquid or gaseous state due to its low volume. Production of hydrogen using photocatalysis is more expensive because of the energy needed to store the gas. Nanomaterial composites and their many modifications are able to efficiently overcome the existing restrictions that hinder photocatalytic performance. This review recommends mixing GFRGO with a combination of boron-nitride and graphene for enhanced performance. In addition, considerations regarding the toxicity of nanomaterials must be considered prior to the adoption of any creative solution. Guidelines and protocols controlling the correct application and management of nanomaterials should be developed in order to reduce the adverse effects on environmental health caused by the toxicity and hazardous effects of the chemical components used. The cost and widespread commercialization of nanomaterials must also be evaluated in order to fulfill expanding energy demands.
